sources: 2026-06-30 bans: 'appetite suppressant', 'real fruit', 'made in USA'/US-manufacturing, 'dye free', 'natural flavors'/'no artificial dyes'
2026-07-02 (Tomas): core bags use sucralose + erythritol, NOT allulose — any allulose claim is false
_meta.note: Severity HARD = definite violation (counts toward violation_rate). WARN = review-before-publish (does not fail the script but is reported). Patterns are case-insensitive regex, matched on word boundaries. Edit this file as policy evolves; the scorer reads it as data. 2026-07-03: appetite suppressant moved from allow-list to HARD ban; added real_fruit, made_in_usa (exception 'packed in the US'), false_ingredient_allulose, and dye-free/natural-flavors/no-artificial-dyes to false_clean_label. '70-90 calories' remains ALLOWED (some bags are 90 cal).

hard.why: Named GLP-1 drug — FDA warning-letter zone
Formulation does not support it (Tomas 2026-06-08; dye-free/natural-flavors/no-artificial-dyes banned 2026-06-30)
Disease/medical claim (verb requires a disease object so the candy noun 'treats' does not false-flag)
Clinical/outcome-guarantee weight claim (narrative weight loss IS allowed — see allow list)
Never tell a distressed customer to keep eating
Drug-effect appetite-suppressant claim (banned 2026-06-30; moved off allow-list)
No real fruit in the formulation (banned 2026-06-30)
Unsubstantiated US-manufacturing claim (banned 2026-06-30; exception: 'packed in the US')
Core bags use sucralose + erythritol, not allulose (Tomas 2026-07-02) — allulose claim is false

warn.why: Bare GLP-1 mention — ride the audience by symptom, not the drug term
Blood-sugar language needs the structure-function disclaimer
Symptom language OK colloquially but review medical framing

allow._why: Permitted language that LOOKS risky but is explicitly approved. The scorer suppresses any HARD/WARN hit whose matched span is inside one of these phrases, preventing false flags on canon-approved copy.
patterns: supports a healthy blood sugar response
i'?ve been trying to lose weight
